description: A signed, chained, Merkle-verifiable audit trail. Every action gets one SHA3-256 hash, one Ed25519 signature, one chain link, one Merkle proof — and the whole thing verifies in any language with crypto primitives. No trusted audit service required.

records.summary: An agent was spawned. Real Ed25519 keypair, real SHA3-256 digest, real Merkle path against the chain root.
records.aftermath: The new agent's public key is added to the trust store and the genesis event hash becomes the anchor that every subsequent action chains off. Hot tier: in-memory log + write-ahead log. Anyone connecting later can ask for the genesis event and verify the public key matches what's expected.

beats.copy: Event 1 of 5. An agent was spawned — the genesis event. Every audit trail starts
somewhere. No predecessor here, just a public key and a starting hash.
Genesis event. agent_id "agent-orchestrator" is being created. Payload hash
0eb9efc80f67… commits to the spawn parameters. The arguments stay in the host system
— only the hash chains.
Encode to canonical bytes: 174 bytes. Domain tag PROV_EVENT_V1, schema version 1,
length-prefixed strings, metadata sorted by key. Re-encoding the same event always
produces these exact bytes.
SHA3-256 of the canonical bytes: 9c8aaa3c248546…. Ed25519 signs that hash with the
agent's private key → 64-byte signature. The 32-byte public key is attached so
anyone can verify without contacting the signer.
Genesis event. prev_event_hash is null — no predecessor. The trace starts here.
Every later event chains back to this.
Merkle proof: 3 sibling hashes (~96 bytes). Walking those siblings reconstructs the
root e38439a06313…. Proof size stays small even as the chain grows.
Public key 0d49ff0d4743…, root e38439a06313…. Signature verifies ✓, chain link clean
✓, Merkle proof against root ✓. Three independent checks — all local, all crypto. No
"audit service" in the loop to trust.

records.summary: The agent read a file. Real Ed25519 keypair, real SHA3-256 digest, real Merkle path against the chain root.
records.aftermath: The read is logged with the file path + byte count, but the file contents are never in the chain — only their hash would be, if we needed to prove what the agent saw. For now the event tells the auditor: this agent, at this position in the trace, read this path. Nothing more, nothing less.

beats.copy: Event 2 of 5. The agent read a file. Every tool call becomes a signed event. The
signature names the agent; the chain link places it in time.
tool.executed from agent-tools. File path and byte count go into metadata, and
payload hash 291b1cfc1328… is what gets signed and chained.
Encode to canonical bytes: 237 bytes. Domain tag PROV_EVENT_V1, schema version 1,
length-prefixed strings, metadata sorted by key. Re-encoding the same event always
produces these exact bytes.
Hash once with SHA3-256, sign with Ed25519, attach public key. 96 bytes of crypto
material per event (32 + 64 + 32) — a one-time fixed cost regardless of payload
size.
prev_event_hash = 9c8aaa3c248546… (leaf hash of event 1). The chain advances.
Reordering or inserting an event breaks this link — the next signature won't match.
Merkle proof: 3 sibling hashes (~96 bytes). Walking those siblings reconstructs the
root e38439a06313…. Proof size stays small even as the chain grows.
Public key 2d6e3d8280f2…, root e38439a06313…. Signature verifies ✓, chain link clean
✓, Merkle proof against root ✓. Three independent checks — all local, all crypto. No
"audit service" in the loop to trust.

records.summary: The agent wrote a file. Real Ed25519 keypair, real SHA3-256 digest, real Merkle path against the chain root.
records.aftermath: The output file's content hash is included in the payload hash, so anyone with the file can verify the agent produced this exact bytes — not a modified version. Hot tier holds the event for ~7 days before rotating to warm storage; the Merkle root persists indefinitely.

beats.copy: Event 3 of 5. The agent wrote a file. Writes get the same treatment as reads — one
signed event, one chain link, one Merkle leaf. Same cost.
Same agent (agent-tools), different tool. Each tool call gets its own payload hash,
metadata, and Merkle leaf — regardless of whether it reads or writes.
Encode to canonical bytes: 234 bytes. The first ~32 bytes match the previous event
(same domain tag + schema version + agent_id prefix) — correct and deterministic,
not a copy-paste. The rest diverge based on event_type, payload, and metadata.
Signature 6b3f5d685cca42… is a real Ed25519 signature, not a hash truncation or
placeholder. Verifiable with curve25519 in any language, including the one running
in your browser.
prev_event_hash = 9129fc5936865e… (leaf hash of event 2). The chain advances.
Reordering or inserting an event breaks this link — the next signature won't match.
Merkle proof: 3 sibling hashes (~96 bytes). Walking those siblings reconstructs the
root e38439a06313…. Proof size stays small even as the chain grows.
Public key 2d6e3d8280f2…, root e38439a06313…. Signature verifies ✓, chain link clean
✓, Merkle proof against root ✓. Three independent checks — all local, all crypto. No
"audit service" in the loop to trust.

records.summary: A tool call failed. Real Ed25519 keypair, real SHA3-256 digest, real Merkle path against the chain root.
records.aftermath: Failures are preserved in the chain with their error trace — the orchestrator can later replay the failed call to debug or analyze retry patterns. Cold tier archives keep failure events alongside successes; there's no separate "good events" log to selectively trust.

beats.copy: Event 4 of 5. A tool call failed. Failures get the same audit treatment as
successes. Every event is logged — an after-the-fact narrative can't quietly omit
the parts that went wrong.
tool.failed — failures get the full treatment too. error_code and timeout are in
metadata, signed alongside everything else. No "happy path" log to selectively
trust.
Encode to canonical bytes: 252 bytes. The first ~32 bytes match the previous event
(same domain tag + schema version + agent_id prefix) — correct and deterministic,
not a copy-paste. The rest diverge based on event_type, payload, and metadata.
Even though this tool call failed, the failure event gets the same SHA3-256 +
Ed25519 + public-key bundle. Failures are first-class citizens of the chain; you
can't selectively skip signing the embarrassing ones.
prev_event_hash = c08c0af1017b81… (leaf hash of event 3). The chain advances.
Reordering or inserting an event breaks this link — the next signature won't match.
Merkle proof: 3 sibling hashes (~96 bytes). Walking those siblings reconstructs the
root e38439a06313…. Proof size stays small even as the chain grows.
Public key 2d6e3d8280f2…, root e38439a06313…. Signature verifies ✓, chain link clean
✓, Merkle proof against root ✓. Three independent checks — all local, all crypto. No
"audit service" in the loop to trust.

records.plain: the agent despawned — then someone tampered with the record
records.summary: Signed honestly, then tampered with after the fact. The signature exposes the lie — anyone can verify this independently, with no audit service in the loop.
records.aftermath: The orchestrator's auditor flags the event on the next verify pass. The trace is preserved with the tamper recorded — the chain doesn't censor failed verifications, it just makes them undeniable. Downstream systems can decide what to do; the cryptographic fact is unambiguous.

beats.copy: Event 5 of 5. The agent despawned — then someone tampered with the record. This
event was signed honestly, then metadata was mutated after the fact. Signature
verification catches it.
agent.despawn from the orchestrator. Metadata claims 3 tools ran (we'll come back to
that). Payload hash 38554786e330… commits to the original event bytes. The signature
lives or dies on whether those bytes change.
Encode to canonical bytes: 209 bytes. Domain tag PROV_EVENT_V1, schema version 1,
length-prefixed strings, metadata sorted by key. Re-encoding the same event always
produces these exact bytes.
Ed25519 signs over the original canonical bytes (before any tampering). That
signature commits to a specific byte sequence — changing any field after the fact
invalidates the signature even if the chain link still matches.
prev_event_hash = fb5791691eac3d… (leaf hash of event 4). The chain advances.
Reordering or inserting an event breaks this link — the next signature won't match.
Merkle proof: 3 sibling hashes (~96 bytes). 2 levels had odd node counts, so the
last entry is paired with itself (the "duplicate-last-for-odd" rule). The verifier
walks each level from leaf to root.
This event was signed honestly. Then someone changed "tools_run" from "3" to "30"
after the fact. The signature was made over the original bytes — the new bytes are
not what got signed. Result: signature FAILS to verify ✗. Merkle proof and chain
link still pass — they prove position, not content. The signature is what catches
the content tamper. One failure is enough.
